2632-5012732026Sleep advances : a journal of the Sleep Research SocietySleep AdvTransoral neuromuscular electrical stimulation therapy adherence and intensity levels in veterans with mild OSA.zpag061zpag061zpag06110.1093/sleepadvances/zpag061To describe the use of transoral neuromuscular stimulation therapy in Veterans with mild obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) by looking at session-level data for both adherence and stimulation intensity.A retrospective review of session-level data of the 82 patients prescribed the eXciteOSA device at the VA San Diego Healthcare System was analyzed. The device is prescribed for use over two phases: (1) Phase 1: one daily 20-min session daily for 42 days, and (2) Phase 2: one daily 20-min session on 2 days per week.Forty-one out of 76 patients (54 per cent) completed Phase 1 (42 sessions) within the first 60 days of using eXciteOSA therapy. Three (4 per cent) completed Phase 1 after the first 60 days, and 32 (42 per cent) patients never completed Phase 1. Initial Phase 1 graduates had a cumulative usage of 866 min, while those that did not complete Phase 1 had 578 min. Average stimulation intensity levels (SIL) in the initial Phase 1 graduates increased from week 1 to week 6 (from 3.2 to 11.2).Based on the prescription-based adherence definition, adherence is modest but is contingent on how adherence is defined. Notably however, median positive airway pressure adherence is 40 per cent for those with a recent prescription within our facility for comparison. Future research should focus on interventions to help improve both adherence and SIL of this novel daytime OSA therapy.Published by Oxford University Press on behalf of Sleep Research Society 2026.StepnowskyCarlCHealth Services Research & Development, VA San Diego Healthcare System, San Diego, CA, United States.Department of Medicine, Division of General Internal Medicine, University of California San Diego, La Jolla, CA, United States.ZamoraTaniaTHealth Services Research & Development, VA San Diego Healthcare System, San Diego, CA, United States.VelazquezYzabelYHealth Services Research & Development, VA San Diego Healthcare System, San Diego, CA, United States.SchmicklChris NCN0000-0001-5417-9980Sleep Medicine Section, VA San Diego Healthcare System, San Diego, CA, United States.Cervantes-GamerosCesarCSleep Medicine Section, VA San Diego Healthcare System, San Diego, CA, United States.OwensRobert LRLSleep Medicine Section, VA San Diego Healthcare System, San Diego, CA, United States.Department of Medicine, Division of Pulmonary, Critical Care, and Sleep Medicine, University of California at San Diego, La Jolla, CA, United States.WojeckBrian SBS0000-0001-9771-1332Department of Endocrinology, Yale University, New Haven, CT, United States.ColvonenPeterPVA Center of Excellence for Stress and Mental Health (CESAMH), San Diego, CA, United States.NokesBrandonB0000-0002-0436-7411Sleep Medicine Section, VA San Diego Healthcare System, San Diego, CA, United States.Department of Medicine, Division of Pulmonary, Critical Care, and Sleep Medicine, University of California at San Diego, La Jolla, CA, United States.engJournal Article20260613
